questions.question: Tell me about yourself
questions.answer:
Structure your answer as: Present → Past → Future (2-3 minutes max).

Template:

"I'm a .NET developer with about 5 years of experience, currently [role/looking for]. I work primarily with ASP.NET Core, Angular, and SQL Server — building web applications from the API layer all the way to the frontend.

Before that, I worked on [briefly mention key projects/responsibilities]. One of the projects I'm most proud of is [FolioTrack/offline invoice/etc.] where I built [specific feature] that [specific impact].

I'm now looking for [type of role] where I can [growth goal — work on larger scale systems / deepen expertise in cloud / lead technically]. I'm particularly interested in this role because [specific reason about the company/tech stack]."

Key principles:

  • Keep it professional — no personal life details unless relevant
  • Lead with current state, not your whole career history
  • Mention 1-2 specific achievements (with numbers if possible)
  • End with why you're here — connects you to this company
  • Practice until it's smooth — don't read from a script but don't ramble
followUps: Why are you looking to change jobs?
What are you looking for in your next role?
questions.tip: Customize the ending for each company. Research the tech stack, team size, product, and mention specifically why this company/role aligns with your goals. Generic "I want to grow" endings are forgettable.

questions.question: Tell me about a challenging project you worked on and how you handled it
questions.answer:
Use the STAR framework: Situation → Task → Action → Result.

Example answer (PaymentReminder project):

Situation: We had a PaymentReminder background service in .NET 8 that was supposed to send reminder emails to clients with overdue invoices. The service worked fine in dev but in production it was occasionally sending duplicate reminders to the same client — causing client complaints.

Task: I needed to identify why duplicates were happening and fix it without disrupting the service (it ran every hour).

Action: I added structured logging (Serilog) to trace each email send with the invoice ID and timestamp. The logs revealed the issue: when two server instances ran simultaneously (after a scale-out), both would pick up the same invoices from the DB because there was no distributed lock. I implemented an idempotency approach — marking invoices as "reminder-sent" in a DB update within the same transaction as the email send. I also added a unique constraint on the reminder tracking table so duplicate records were impossible at the DB level.

Result: Zero duplicate reminders in the 4 months since the fix. The extra logging also helped us catch other issues faster.
followUps: What did you learn from it?
questions.tip: STAR answers should be specific, not generic. "We had a bug, I fixed it" is not a story. The interviewer wants to understand your problem-solving process, how you communicate under pressure, and how you handle ambiguity.

questions.question: Tell me about a conflict you had with a team member and how you resolved it
questions.answer:
Use STAR. Show maturity — you focused on the problem, not the person.

Key principles for conflict questions:

  • Never blame the other person — "they were wrong, I was right" is a red flag
  • Show you took initiative to resolve it, didn't wait for a manager
  • Focus on the outcome and what you both learned
  • Demonstrate empathy — you understood their perspective

Example structure:

"In [project], I disagreed with a colleague about [technical/process decision]. They wanted to [their approach], while I believed [your approach] was better because [technical reason].

Instead of escalating immediately, I set up a short one-on-one to understand their reasoning. It turned out they had [context I didn't know about — deadline pressure, constraint]. Once I understood their concern, we agreed to [compromise or you adjusted your view].

The outcome was [result]. I learned that [what you took away — importance of understanding context before disagreeing, how to frame technical disagreements as options with tradeoffs rather than right/wrong]."
followUps: How do you handle disagreements about technical decisions with senior developers?
What do you do when you strongly disagree with a decision but are outvoted?
questions.tip: "Disagree and commit" is a key phrase. If you voiced your concern clearly and the team decided otherwise, you commit fully to executing the decision. Passive resistance ("fine, we'll do it your way") is a red flag. Show you can advocate for your view and then move forward.

questions.question: Where do you see yourself in 5 years?
questions.answer:
Interviewers want to know: Are you serious about growing? Will you stay long enough to be worth hiring? Are your goals realistic?

Good answer structure:

"In 5 years, I see myself as a senior or lead .NET developer — someone who's not just writing code but helping define architecture decisions, mentoring junior developers, and having a technical influence on the product direction.

Short-term, in the next 1-2 years, I want to deepen my expertise in [cloud-native development / microservices / system design] and contribute to more complex, higher-scale systems than I've worked on so far.

Longer-term, I'm open to either a technical track (principal engineer, architect) or a hybrid technical lead role — I want to stay hands-on with code but also have more impact on the system design and team direction. This role seems like a great step toward that because [specific mention of what this company offers]."

Avoid:

  • "In 5 years I want your job" — presumptuous
  • "I want to be a manager" — unless they're hiring for that path
  • "I'm not sure" — signals lack of direction
followUps: What are your short-term career goals?
What skills are you actively developing?
questions.tip: Tie your 5-year goal to this role. Show that this job is a step toward your goal, not just a placeholder. This tells the interviewer you've thought about your career and that this role genuinely serves it.

questions.question: What is your greatest strength?
questions.answer:
Pick a strength that is: real (you can prove it), relevant to the role, and specific (not generic).

Bad answer: "I'm a hard worker and a good team player." (Everyone says this.)

Good answer structure:

"One of my strongest skills is [specific technical/problem-solving skill]. For example, in [project], I was able to [specific situation]. I approached it by [what you did], which resulted in [specific outcome].

More broadly, I'm good at [meta-skill — breaking down complex problems / communicating technical concepts to non-technical stakeholders / picking up new technologies quickly] and that's been valuable in projects like [brief reference]."

Strengths relevant for .NET developers:

  • Debugging and root-cause analysis
  • Translating business requirements into clean technical designs
  • Picking up new technologies quickly (Angular, Azure, etc.)
  • Writing maintainable, well-structured code
  • Performance optimization instincts
followUps: Can you give me a specific example of that strength?
questions.tip: Greatest weakness follow-up: Pick a real weakness you're actively working on — not a disguised strength ("I work too hard"). Example: "I sometimes spend too much time optimizing code before shipping. I've been working on recognizing when 'good enough' is the right call for the current stage."

questions.question: How do you handle tight deadlines or pressure?
questions.answer:
Interviewers want to know you don't panic, you communicate, and you make smart tradeoffs.

Good answer:

"When I'm under a tight deadline, the first thing I do is prioritize ruthlessly — what absolutely must ship vs what's nice-to-have. I've learned that trying to do everything at 80% quality is worse than doing the critical parts at 100% and deferring the rest.

I also communicate early. If I see a deadline is at risk, I tell the team or manager as soon as I'm aware — not the day before. Early warning gives us options (scope reduction, extra help, deadline extension). Silence until the last minute doesn't help anyone.

In [specific project], we had a tight deadline where [situation]. I [specific action — broke it down, cut scope, worked with the team]. We delivered [result — what shipped, what was deferred and why that was the right call].

The one thing I try to avoid under pressure is cutting corners on code quality in ways that create tech debt that takes 3x longer to fix later. I'd rather have a productive conversation about scope than ship something fragile."
followUps: Tell me about a time you missed a deadline.
questions.tip: If you missed a deadline: Own it, explain what happened, what you learned, and what you'd do differently. Don't blame external factors. Accountability and learning from mistakes is valued more than perfection.

questions.question: Why do you want to work here?
questions.answer:
This question tests: Did you research us? Do you actually want this job or are we just option #12?

Answer formula: Company-specific reason + Role-specific fit + Your goal alignment

Research angles:

  • Tech stack — "I've been using .NET and SQL Server for 5 years, and I see your team is building on these. I can contribute immediately while also learning [their specific tech]."
  • Product — "I've been following [company's product] for a while. The [specific feature/challenge] is technically interesting to me because [genuine reason]."
  • Team/culture — "The engineering blog post about [specific topic] showed how seriously you take [code quality / engineering culture / specific practice]. That's the kind of team I want to work with."
  • Scale/challenge — "The scale at which you operate [millions of users, high throughput] is the kind of engineering challenge I want to work on next."

What to avoid:

  • "You pay well" (even if true)
  • "It's close to my home"
  • Generic "great company culture" without specifics
followUps: What do you know about our product/company?
What makes you a better fit than other candidates?
questions.tip: Spend 30 minutes researching before every interview. LinkedIn, company engineering blog, Glassdoor, recent news. Mention something specific you found. It takes candidates from forgettable to memorable.

questions.question: How do you keep yourself updated with new .NET technologies?
questions.answer:
This tests whether you're passionate about the craft and proactive about growth.

Genuine sources I follow:

  • .NET Blog (dotnet.microsoft.com/blog) — official announcements and deep dives
  • Scott Hanselman's blog — practical .NET tips from inside Microsoft
  • YouTube: Nick Chapsas (clean architecture, new features), Tim Corey, Raw Coding
  • Microsoft Learn / docs.microsoft.com — official documentation for new APIs
  • GitHub: Follow .NET runtime repo, ASP.NET Core repo — see what's coming in preview
  • Dev.to, Medium — community articles about real-world usage

Active learning methods:

  • Build small side projects with new features (that's how I tried out Minimal APIs and .NET 8's primary constructors)
  • Contribute to personal projects to experiment with new patterns
  • Read source code of popular libraries (I've learned a lot reading Polly's and EF Core's internals)

"The .NET release cycle (one major version per year) gives a natural rhythm — I always spend time with the What's New page for each release and try the highlighted features in a sandbox project."
followUps: What's a recent .NET feature you're excited about?
What would you build if you had unlimited time?
questions.tip: Name a specific recent .NET feature — .NET 9 AOT, C# 12 primary constructors, interceptors, .NET Aspire, Blazor SSR, etc. Having a concrete example shows you actually follow the ecosystem, not just claim to.
